story.intro: For centuries, mathematicians calculated areas under curves one sliver at a time. Then came two天才 — Barrow and Newton — who proved that differentiation and integration are perfect inverses of each other. Today, you'll feel why this theorem is called the most important link in all of mathematics.
story.theoremStatement: If F(x) = ∫f(x)dx from a to x, then d/dx F(x) = f(x).
story.plainEnglish: The area under a curve and the slope of a curve are two views of the same thing. Finding areas (integration) and finding slopes (differentiation) undo each other — completely. One operation is the reverse of the other.
applications: Physics engines in video games use it to simulate gravity, momentum, and energy realistically
Your phone's battery indicator uses integration to track energy consumption over time
Engineers use it to calculate center of mass, moment of inertia, and structural stress

stages.question: What is the derivative of x²? (d/dx of x²)
stages.hint: Use the power rule: bring the exponent down as a coefficient, then reduce the exponent by 1.
stages.conceptShown: d/dx (x²) = 2x — the power rule in action

stages.question: What is the derivative of a constant? For example, d/dx(7)?
stages.hint: A constant never changes — it has no rate of change. What is the slope of a flat horizontal line?
stages.conceptShown: d/dx(7) = 0 — the derivative of any constant is 0

stages.question: If you integrate (find the area under) the function f(x) = 2x from x = 0 to x = 3, what is the exact area?
stages.hint: The antiderivative of 2x is x². Evaluate from 0 to 3: 3² - 0².

stages.question: What do you call the reverse of differentiation — the process of finding areas under curves?
stages.hint: If differentiation finds slopes, what finds areas under curves? Think of the inverse operation.
stages.conceptShown: Integration (or finding an antiderivative) is the reverse of differentiation

stages.question: According to the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, if F(x) is the integral of f(x), then F'(x) = ?
stages.hint: The theorem states that differentiation and integration are inverse operations. What happens when you differentiate the integral of a function?
stages.conceptShown: F'(x) = f(x) — the integral's derivative is the original function

stages.question: If F(x) = ∫3x dx from 0 to x, what is F'(x)?
stages.hint: The Fundamental Theorem says: differentiate the integral, and you get back the original function. No calculation needed.
stages.conceptShown: F'(x) = 3x — differentiating the integral returns the original integrand

stages.question: Final challenge: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects differentiation and integration as inverse operations. What is d/dx(∫f(x)dx)?
stages.hint: This is literally the statement of the Fundamental Theorem. Integrate a function, then differentiate the result — what do you get back?
stages.conceptShown: d/dx(∫f(x)dx) = f(x) — the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, in one line
